content.problemStatement:
You are given a graph of NNN vertices - numbered from 111 to NNN and MMM edges between them. 

The ithi^{th}ith edge connects the AiA_iAi​ node to BiB_iBi​. The edges are directed.

You live at the vertex 111, output all the vertices that you can visit. You can travel using the given edges.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a two integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of vertices and edges.
Next MMM lines describes the edges. i−thi-thi−th of these MMM lines contains two space-separated integers AiA_iAi​ and BiB_iBi​ denoting there exists a directed edge from AiA_iAi​ to BiB_iBi​.


Output Format
Output all the nodes you can visit from the node 1, in increasing order of their index.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
The sum of M over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M — the number of vertices and the number of directed edges. The next M lines each contain two integers A and B, denoting a directed edge from vertex A to vertex B.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a single line all vertices that are reachable from vertex 1 (including vertex 1 itself), in increasing order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Starting from vertex 1 we can reach 3, then 2, then 4. Vertices 1,2,3,4 are reachable; vertex 5 is not.
First test case: 1→2→3, so 1 2 3 are reachable. Second test case: there is only an edge 2→3, vertex 1 cannot leave itself, so only 1 is reachable.
No edges exist, therefore only the starting vertex 1 can be visited.

solution.approach: For each test case we perform a graph traversal starting from vertex 1. Both depth‑first search (DFS) and breadth‑first search (BFS) work because we only need to know which vertices are reachable, not the order of visitation. An iterative stack (DFS) is used to avoid recursion depth limits. We store the adjacency list, mark visited vertices, and after the traversal output all vertices i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) for which visited[i] is true. Since we iterate i in increasing order, the output is automatically sorted.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, because each vertex and each edge is processed at most once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) for the adjacency list and the visited array.
